coredata.dc:title: Exploration of carbon dots derived from epimedium towards detecting dopamine and hydrogen peroxide
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lu
Liu, Jie
Bai, Yunfeng
Feng, Feng
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Herein, an innovative method to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) has been successfully established, and these CDs emitted the yellow fluorescence while epimedium served as the carbon source. To be specific, the CDs prepared here were equipped with the functional groups of -COOH, -NH2, -OH, CC and CO, providing the possibility of functioning as the fluorescent sensor. Importantly, introducing dopamine leaded to their fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared CDs, which was due to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and DA. Meanwhile, the proposed CDs achieved detecting DA with the linear range of 1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−3 M at a detection limit of 8.7 × 10−8 M. Moreover, the further addition of H2O2 gradually resulted in the fluorescence recovering through destroying the electrostatic interaction as the concentration of H2O2 varied from 10−6 to 10−2 M with the detection limit of 7.2 × 10−7 M. Thereby, the CDs described here exhibited the potentiality of broadening the ways for assaying DA and H2O2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Epimedium
Detection
Dopamine
H2O2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimizing the conditions of CDs@Chol detecting DA and H2O2
Building up the assay for CDs detecting DA and H2O2
Mechanism of CDs detecting DA and H2O2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of synthesizing CDs with epimedium for assaying dopamine and H2O2.

(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of CDs, Inset: images of CDs with sunlight and UV; (B) Emission spectra of CDs with different excitations; (C) HR-TEM image, (D) DLS analysis, (E) FTIR spectrum and (F) XPS survey of CDs described here; Deconvolution XPS spectra of C1s (G), N1s (H) and O1s (I).

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (a), with 5 × 10−4 M of DA (b); Fluorescence intensity of CDs with DA for different incubation time (B), temperature (C); Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with 5 × 10−4 M of DA (a), as well as 10−2 M (b) of H2O2 (D); Fluorescence intensity of CDs with DA and H2O2 for different incubation time (E), temperature (F).

Fluorescence decrease (F/F0) of CDs in presence of DA or other compounds (A), CDs with DA in presence of ions or compounds (B); Fluorescence decrease (F/F0) of CDs in presence of DA with H2O2 or other compounds (C), CDs with DA and H2O2 in presence of ions or compounds (D).

(A) Fluorescence variation of CDs with different concentrations of DA; (B) Images of CDs in different concentrations of DA with daylight and UV light; (C) Plot of (F0/F–1) versus different concentrations of DA; (D) Fluorescence variation of CDs and DA with different concentrations of H2O2; (E) Images of CDs and DA in different concentrations of H2O2 with daylight and UV light; (F) Plot of (F/F0–1) versus different concentrations of H2O2.

(A) Absorption of DA, fluorescence excitation and emission of CDs; (B) Zeta potentials of CDs, DA, CDs with DA, CDs in presence of DA and H2O2; (C) Size distributions and (D) FTIR of CDs, CDs with DA, CDs with both DA and H2O2.

head.ce:title: Exploration of carbon dots derived from epimedium towards detecting dopamine and hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi Provincial Key Laboratory of Chemical Biosensing, Shanxi Datong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Shanxi Datong University, Datong 037009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, an innovative method to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) has been successfully established, and these CDs emitted the yellow fluorescence while epimedium served as the carbon source. To be specific, the CDs prepared here were equipped with the functional groups of -COOH, -NH2, -OH, CC and CO, providing the possibility of functioning as the fluorescent sensor. Importantly, introducing dopamine leaded to their fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared CDs, which was due to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and DA. Meanwhile, the proposed CDs achieved detecting DA with the linear range of 1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−3 M at a detection limit of 8.7 × 10−8 M. Moreover, the further addition of H2O2 gradually resulted in the fluorescence recovering through destroying the electrostatic interaction as the concentration of H2O2 varied from 10−6 to 10−2 M with the detection limit of 7.2 × 10−7 M. Thereby, the CDs described here exhibited the potentiality of broadening the ways for assaying DA and H2O2.

Herein, we have successfully established a facile method for synthesizing yellow-carbon dots (CDs) while epimedium served as the carbon source. Specifically, the CDs described here were equipped with the functional groups of -COOH, -NH2, -OH, CC and CO, providing the possibility of functioning as the fluorescent sensor. Importantly, their fluorescence of CDs showed the obvious decrease when dopamine was introduced due to the electrostatic interaction between CDs and dopamine, and thus we achieved employing CDs to detect dopamine with the acceptable linear range and detection limit on the basis of this phenomenon. Besides, the further addition of H2O2 gradually resulted in the fluorescence recovering through destroying the electrostatic interaction. Thereby, the CDs may provide a new strategy of assaying dopamine and H2O2.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), as a member of catecholamine neurotransmitters, mainly functions as the neurotransmitter and hormone for the activities of the central nerve and kidney, especially for the hormone release in brain [1,2]. DA facilitates to enhance the memory and treats Alzheimer's disease [3], and it indeed plays the positive role for the heart surgery, hypertension, bronchial asthma and beyond [4]. However, the abnormal amount of DA is usually closely associated with the neurological diseases including schizophrenia, dementia and Parkinson’s disease [5,6]. Owing to the significance of DA for human health, exploring the convenient methods of determining DA is of great value.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), acting as a crucial metabolite, participates in various biological processes such as biosynthesis, host defense or cellular signal transduction [7,8]. Basically, hydrogen peroxide could penetrate the cellular membranes [9], thus achieving the regulation of the physiological events. Nevertheless, the abuse of hydrogen peroxide may induce the formation of oxidative stress, so resulting in kinds of diseases such as Alzheimer's, asthma, cardiovascular disorders or cancers [10,11]. Thereby, developing the accurate and easier methods to evaluate the level of hydrogen peroxide is meaningful in the multifarious fields.

So far, kinds of techniques mainly including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [12,13], colorimetry [14,15], chemiluminescence [16,17], and electrochemistry [18,19], have been applied to detect DA and hydrogen peroxide. Nevertheless, these existed approaches are more or less restricted by their complicated operations, time-consuming or expensive instrumentation. As been reported, other innovative materials and strategies have been raised to detect DA and H2O2. For instance, quantum dots with the compact surface coatings were applied to examine dopamine through the self-assembled process with DA molecules [20], and reduced graphene oxide/nile blue/gold nanoparticles complex modified carbon electrode was also designed and prepared to sensitively assay DA [21]. Again, a ratiometric fluorescence probe based on CDs and Cu nanoclusters was applied for the dual-emission detection of DA [22]. Also, an assembly approach between black phosphorus quantum dots-doped MOF and silver nanoclusters was developed to assay H2O2 [23].

Since carbon dots emerged as a new member of the carbon nanomaterials, considerable contributions have been devoted to the development of CDs [24,25]. Due to their unique size of less 10 nm, CDs usually show the advanced properties consisting of the favorable fluorescence, acceptable stability, biocompatibility and low cost [26,27]. Therefore, CDs have been intensively utilized in various fields of electronics, photonics, catalysis, biochemistry and beyond, especially for developing sensing probes [28,29]. Fundamentally, carbon dots could be prepared from kinds of carbon-based materials, and their luminescent property is associated with different carbon source and synthesis methods. Accordingly, plenty of raw materials such as the commercial chemicals and natural plants played the role as carbon source [30,31], and lots of CDs directed by natural products emit the short-wavelength fluorescence [30,32]. Thereby, hundreds of approaches including hydrothermal reactions, laser ablation, pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation and microwave treatment have been reported towards synthesizing CDs [33–36].

In this study, a simple and efficient method to synthesizing CDs was built up, and one kind natural product of epimedium originally played the role as the carbon source ( Fig. 1A), providing the current environmental-friendly and low-toxicity synthesis and exhibiting the satisfactory yellow fluorescence compared with the short-wavelength fluorescence. Subsequently, we employed these CDs to assay DA on the basis of the electrostatic interaction between CDs and DA, thus leading to their fluorescence quenching of CDs (Fig. 1B). In addition, the further introduction of H2O2 recovered the quenched fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, we here successfully raised a CDs-based assay for the detections of DA and H2O2.

All the ions (Na+, K+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Cu2+ and Mg2+), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium chloride, pyridoxine, L-Lysine, L-Histidine, vitamin C, L-Cysteine, as well glucose as were purchased from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Similarly, epimedium was obtained from herbal medicine market (Chongqing, China), and dopamine (DA), nicotinamide, cimetidine, omeprazole, thiamine and gentamicin was acquired from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Meanwhile, ranitidine, metformin, ribavirin and glutathione were bought from Sigma Aldrich Shanghai Trading Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and sodium hydroxide was purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Again, clindamycin, tobramycin, erythromycin, isoniazide, nizatidine, theophylline and nicotinic acid were obtained from Shanghai Biological Technology Development Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Additionally, the ultrapure water of 18.25 MΩ·cm was produced by an ultrapure water system of Aquapro AWL-0502-P (Chongqing, China), which was utilized through the whole experimental procedure.

All the fluorescence spectra were performed by using Shimadzu RF-6000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a quartz cell of 1 cm × 1 cm (Tokyo, Japan), while the excitation and emission slit were set at 5/5 nm. Similarly, UV/Vis absorption spectra were achieved by a Shimadzu UV-1750 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and the elemental analyses were accomplished by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Also, the major functional groups were identified using Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Again, Zetasizer Nano dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern England) was introduced to investigate the size distributions of CDs. Meanwhile, the thermostatic water bath (DF-101s) was bought from Gongyi Instrument Co., Ltd. (Gongyi, China). Besides, Leici pHS-3C digital pH meter (Shanghai, China) was introduced for determining the pH value of the solutions, while a vortex of QL-901 (Haimen, China) served for mixing the solutions. Furthermore, the powder of CDs was acquired through lyophilization with PiloFD8–4.3V (Charlotte, USA).

Epimedium, as the carbon resource, was originally applied to prepare CDs here. Briefly, epimedium was ground into powder, and 0.1 g of this powder was added into 4 mL of NaOH solution (2 M) and the mixture was transferred into a teflon stainless autoclave, and the reaction was incubated at 180 °C for 8 h. As the autoclave was cooled to the room temperature, the rude product was dissolved into 4 mL of ultrapure water. Next, the previous solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 8000 rpm to achieve the removal of the undissolved particles, and the supernatant was therewith collected. Finally, the obtained solution was filtered with a 0.22 µm of filter membrane and further purified through the dialysis process (membrane of 1000 MWCO) for 12 h, then was diluted by three times and stored at 4 °C before use.

As a standard procedure, the paralleled CDs solutions (each for 100 μL) and various concentrations of DA (each for 100 μL) were successively pipetted in 1.5 mL of Eppendorf tubes. Subsequently, the reactions above were separately diluted to 1 mL with ultrapure water, and followed by thoroughly mixing with a vortex. Then, these mixtures were incubated to react at 30 °C for 60 min, and subjected to the fluorescence measurement.

Likewise, the determination of H2O2 was achieved based on their fluorescence recovering of CDs. In brief, 100 μL of CDs, 700 μL of ultrapure water and 100 μL of DA (5 ×10−3 M) was continuously added into the paralleled 1.5 mL of Eppendorf tubes, and then 100 μL of H2O2 with various concentrations were separately pipetted into the previous tubes followed by the incubation of 180 min at 40 °C. Finally, their fluorescence variations of the solutions were recorded.

To investigate the luminescence property of the synthesized CDs, their maximum excitation and emission spectra were initially recorded as 495 nm and 560 nm ( Fig. 2A), and the proposed CDs emitted the yellow fluorescence with UV light of 365 nm, while appearing as yellow under daylight (Fig. 2A). To address whether the CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission character or not, their emission spectra upon varying excitation wavelengths were recorded. Briefly, the emission peak shifted to the longer wavelength and its corresponding fluorescence intensity decreased along with the excitation wavelengths varying from 455 nm to 505 nm, indicating that their emissions of CDs depended the related excitations (Fig. 2B).

To explore their surface morphology of the prepared CDs, HR-TEM and DLS analyses were performed and these CDs were well-dispersed and emerged within an average diameter of 8.6 ± 0.8 nm (the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm) (Fig. 2C and D). For characterizing the functional groups and structure of the CDs, FTIR was employed (Fig. 2E). In particular, the FTIR spectrum showed the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H at 3473 cm−1, while the stretching vibration of CN and of CO appeared at 1643 cm−1 as well as the stretching vibration of C-N at 1416 cm−1. To identify the components of the current CDs, XPS survey was further performed (Fig. 2F), and three major elements of C1s, O1s and N1s appeared in XPS spectrum. To be specific, the deconvolution of the C1s spectrum indicated six types of carbon bonds as C-C/CC (284.6 eV), C-N/CN (285.3 eV) and OC-O (288.4 eV) (Fig. 2G). Similarly, the N1s spectrum showed the nitrogen signals of C-NC at 399.5 eV and N-C3 at 400.4 eV (Fig. 2H). Again, the oxygen signals of the O1s spectrum revealed the existence of CO at 530.8 eV, C-O at 531.7 eV and -COOH/H2O at 538.8 eV (Fig. 2I). Taken together, these findings demonstrated that the as-prepared CDs are mainly composed of C, N and O with the functional groups of -COOH, -NH2, -OH, CC and CO [37].

For obtaining the optimal conditions of CDs synthesis, the related experiments were performed. The fluorescence intensity of CDs varied along with varied amounts of epimedium, NaOH, the reaction time and temperature (Fig. S1A, S1B, S1C and S1D), demonstrating that synthesizing CDs was regulated by these reaction conditions. Thereby, 0.1 g powder of epimedium, 2.0 M of NaOH, 180 °C and 8 h served as the optimized conditions for preparing CDs.

To evaluate the fluorescence property of CDs, their fluorescence variation was explored through the corresponding experiments. As described in Fig. S2A-S2D, the fluorescent intensity of CDs exhibited little variation accompanied by varying time, temperature, concentrations of NaCl and pH, indicating their generally acceptable stability of the proposed CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the conditions of CDs@Chol detecting DA and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: Considering their advanced fluorescence of the CDs prepared here, we addressed whether they showed the potential as a fluorescent probe or not. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence decrease of CDs was obviously observed with 5 × 10−4 M of DA compared with that in absence of DA (Fig. 3A) under UV light. To acquire the optimized conditions of CDs assaying DA, various possible factors that may affect the detections were investigated. Towards the possible influence of the incubation time on the detecting process, varying time (10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 40 min, 50 min, 60 min, 70 min, 80 min and 90 min) for CDs reacting with DA were identified (Fig. 3B). Similarly, the reaction temperature ranging from 20° to 55°C were further studied (Fig. 3C). Accordingly, 60 min and 30 °C were selected as the optimized conditions. More importantly, the further introduction of H2O2 (1 ×10−2 M) could recover their quenched fluorescence of CDs (Fig. 3D). Similarly, the conditions of CDs sensing H2O2 were also identified through the incubated time and temperature. Hence, 180 min and 40 °C served as the optimal conditions for CDs detecting H2O2 (Fig. 3E and F). Again, all the subsequent experiments were followed these conditions.

To identify the selectivity of CDs assaying DA, 100 μL of multiple medicines (10−2 M) including glutathione, nicotinamide, nicotinic acid, thiamine, tobramycin, omeprazole, ranitidine and erythromycin were introduced. Compared with DA, the other medicines here showed scarce effect to the fluorescence of CDs ( Fig. 4A), revealing the generally acceptable selectivity of CDs detecting DA. Then, the interference by the possible coexisting ions (Ba2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+ and Mn2+) and drugs (ribavirin, clindamycin, metformin, cimetidine and isoniazide) were examined while CDs sensing DA, and each was added with 100 μL of 10−2 M. As exhibited in Fig. 4B, the additions of these ions and drugs hardly affected the process of CDs detecting DA, which suggested the satisfactory anti-interference.

Likewise, the selectivity for assaying H2O2 by CDs was also explored. As illustrated in Fig. 4C, H2O2 rather than other drugs (vitamin C, nizatidine, L-Cysteine, glucose, theophylline, L-Lysine, nicotinamide and thiamine) obviously recovered their fluorescence of CDs. Also, the possibly coexisting ions and drugs (Na+, K+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, gentamicin, L-Histidine, pyridoxine, omeprazole, glutathione, isoniazide, nicotinic acid and thiamine) were added into the system of CDs with both DA and H2O2 (Fig. 4D), demonstrating the favorable anti-interference ability of the current assay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building up the assay for CDs detecting DA and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: By virtue of their fluorescence quenching and recovery of CDs, the assaying strategy towards DA and H2O2 was successfully established. Consequently, the calibration curve was built upon the optimal conditions and the fluorescence of CDs showed the corresponding decrease as the concentrations of dopamine increasing from 1.0 × 10−7 to 1.0 × 10−3 M ( Fig. 5A). Meanwhile, the related images for CDs with different concentrations of DA under daylight and UV were acquired (Fig. 5B). Besides, the fluorescence intensity decrease (F0/F-1) versus the concentrations of dopamine displayed a linear relationship with a coefficient of 0.981 (Fig. 5C), while the detection limit for dopamine was 8.7 × 10−8 M at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3. Furthermore, the addition of H2O2 into CDs with DA leaded to their gradual fluorescence recovering, and the fluorescence variation versus the concentrations of H2O2 displayed the linear relationship from 10−6 to 10−2 M with a detection limit of 7.2 × 10−7 M (Fig. 5D and F). Simultaneously, the related optical change with the daylight or UV was also recorded by imaging (Fig. 5E). Therefore, these findings suggested that a facile approach of CDs assaying dopamine and H2O2 was successfully established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of CDs detecting DA and H2O2
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the mechanism of CDs detecting DA based on their fluorescence quenching, the further experiments were performed. Primarily, the absorption spectrum of DA scarcely showed the overlapping region with the fluorescent excitation and emission of CDs ( Fig. 6A), demonstrating that the quenching was not originated from inner filter effect (IFE). Additionally, DA was identified as positively charged with 3.55 mV while CDs with a negative charge of − 28.0 mV, and the CDs with DA exhibited a negative charge of − 16.3 mV (Fig. 6B), demonstrating that there occurred the electrostatic interaction between CDs and DA. Further, the DLS data of CDs showed the growing diameter (17.8 ± 1.2 nm) with the addition of DA compared with that (8.6 ± 0.8 nm) of the single CDs (Fig. 6C), thus resulting in their fluorescence quenching of CDs. According to the FTIR, the addition of DA described the formation of hydrogen bond between CDs and DA (Fig. 6D), further promoting the electrostatic interaction of CDs with DA. Besides, the introduction of H2O2 into the system of CDs with DA showed the negative charge of − 25.8 mV (Fig. 6B) and an average size-distribution of 13.2 ± 1.0 nm (Fig. 6C), may destroying the interaction of CDs and DA, thus recovering their fluorescence of CDs.

In summary, we have originally synthesized carbon dots with epimedium through a facile and green approach, and these CDs exhibited the generally satisfactory yellow fluorescence. Importantly, the current CDs could be utilized for the quantification of DA and H2O2 based on their fluorescence quenching and recovering of CDs. The corresponding mechanism was studied by the electrostatic interaction between CDs and DA, which contributed to the size growth and Zeta potential change of CDs, thus facilitating to quench the fluorescence of CDs. Overall, the raised CDs here may provide the new way for monitoring the amount of DA and H2O2.

Lu Zhao: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Jie Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yunfeng Bai: Resources, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoming Yang: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Feng Feng: Conceptualization, Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi (201801D121035), Cultivate Scientific Research Excellence Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (2020KJ023), Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (2020–133).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127179.

ce:section.ce:para:

.
